annotations.label: Pythagorean triple

anchor.snippet: A Pythagorean triple consists of three positive integers

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `PythagoreanTriple x y z := x*x + y*y = z*z` over `ℤ`, a generalization of the positive-integer Wikipedia notion.

annotations.label: (3, 4, 5) is a Pythagorean triple

anchor.snippet: a well-known example is (3, 4, 5)

annotations.note: No standalone lemma instantiating `PythagoreanTriple 3 4 5` was found.

annotations.label: Scaling a Pythagorean triple

anchor.snippet: If ( a , b , c ) is a Pythagorean triple, then so is ( ka , kb , kc )

annotations.note: `PythagoreanTriple.mul` shows scaling by `k` preserves the triple; `mul_iff` gives the iff form.

annotations.label: Pythagorean triangle

anchor.snippet: A triangle whose side lengths are a Pythagorean triple is a right triangle and called a Pythagorean triangle

annotations.note: Mathlib has no separate `PythagoreanTriangle` definition; only the integer-triple predicate `PythagoreanTriple`.

annotations.label: Primitive Pythagorean triple

anchor.snippet: A primitive Pythagorean triple is one in which a , b and c are coprime

annotations.note: There is no standalone `PrimitivePythagoreanTriple` definition; primitivity is expressed as `PythagoreanTriple x y z ∧ Int.gcd x y = 1`.

annotations.label: (3,4,5) primitive vs (6,8,10) not

anchor.snippet: (3, 4, 5) is a primitive Pythagorean triple whereas (6, 8, 10) is not

annotations.note: No concrete primitivity examples for (3,4,5) or (6,8,10) are stated.

annotations.label: Every triple scales to a unique primitive

anchor.snippet: Every Pythagorean triple can be scaled to a unique primitive Pythagorean triple

annotations.note: `PythagoreanTriple.normalize` divides through by `gcd x y` to produce a coprime triple, but uniqueness is not packaged as a single statement.

annotations.label: Pythagorean theorem

anchor.snippet: stating that every right triangle has side lengths satisfying the formula

annotations.note: The Pythagorean identity is stated for inner product spaces at orthogonal vectors (vector and angle forms).

annotations.label: Non-integer right triangle

anchor.snippet: the triangle with sides

annotations.note: No specific non-integer right-triangle example is formalized.

annotations.label: Diophantine equation

anchor.snippet: is a Diophantine equation

annotations.note: This is descriptive context, not a statable proposition.

annotations.label: 16 primitive triples up to 100

anchor.snippet: There are 16 primitive Pythagorean triples of numbers up to 100

annotations.note: No count of primitive triples below a bound is formalized.

annotations.label: Euclid's formula

anchor.snippet: Euclid's formula

annotations.note: `PythagoreanTriple.classification` gives the iff form parametrizing all triples by `(k, m, n)`.

annotations.label: Primitivity condition in Euclid's formula

anchor.snippet: The triple generated by Euclid 's formula is primitive if and only if m and n are coprime and exactly one of them is even

annotations.note: `IsPrimitiveClassified` and `coprime_classification` encode coprimality of `m, n` plus the opposite-parity condition.

annotations.label: Every primitive triple from coprime pair

anchor.snippet: Every primitive triple arises (after the exchange of a and b , if a is even) from a unique pair of coprime numbers

annotations.note: The iff statement of `coprime_classification` realizes every coprime triple via Euclid's formula on a coprime opposite-parity pair `(m, n)`.

annotations.label: Infinitely many primitive triples

anchor.snippet: It follows that there are infinitely many primitive Pythagorean triples

annotations.note: Infinitude of primitive triples is not stated as a Mathlib lemma.

annotations.label: Extended formula with parameter k

anchor.snippet: every Pythagorean triple is generated uniquely by

annotations.note: `classification` parametrizes every triple by `k, m, n` (uniqueness modulo sign is not explicitly singled out).

annotations.label: Consecutive Pell numbers give a,b differing by 1

anchor.snippet: if m and n are consecutive Pell numbers , a and b will differ by 1

annotations.note: No lemma linking consecutive Pell numbers to leg-difference-1 triples is present.

annotations.label: Sufficiency of Euclid's formula

anchor.snippet: That satisfaction of Euclid's formula by a, b, c is sufficient for the triangle to be Pythagorean

annotations.note: The backward direction of `classification` provides sufficiency, proved directly by `ring`.

annotations.label: Necessity of Euclid's formula

anchor.snippet: A proof of the necessity that a, b, c be expressed by Euclid's formula

annotations.note: `PythagoreanTriple.classified` (and `isPrimitiveClassified_of_coprime`) establishes the necessity direction via the circle parametrization.

annotations.label: Interpretation of parameters

anchor.section: Interpretation of parameters in Euclid's formula
anchor.snippet: Suppose the sides of a Pythagorean triangle have lengths

annotations.note: The geometric interpretation of `m, n` is not formalized.

annotations.label: Symmetric variant of Euclid's formula

anchor.snippet: If m and n are two odd integers such that m > n

annotations.note: The odd-odd symmetric variant of Euclid's formula is not formalized.

annotations.label: Merged unique formula without exchange

anchor.snippet: Every primitive Pythagorean triple can be uniquely written

annotations.note: The non-exchange unique parametrization is not formalized.

annotations.label: Perfect square property

anchor.snippet: is always a perfect square

annotations.note: No specific perfect-square identity about Pythagorean triples is found.

annotations.label: At most one square among a,b,c

anchor.snippet: At most one of a , b , c is a square

annotations.note: This property is not stated as a Mathlib lemma.

annotations.label: Area not a square or twice a square

anchor.snippet: The area of a Pythagorean triangle cannot be the square

annotations.note: Fermat's right-triangle theorem (the underlying fact) is essentially `FermatLastTheoremFor 4`; the precise area statement isn't isolated.

annotations.label: Exactly one leg is even, hypotenuse odd

anchor.snippet: Exactly one of a , b is divisible by 2 (is even ), and the hypotenuse c is always odd

annotations.note: `even_odd_of_coprime` shows exactly one of the legs is even in a primitive triple; oddness of `c` is implicit but not stated as its own lemma.

annotations.label: Exactly one leg divisible by 3

anchor.snippet: Exactly one of a , b is divisible by 3, but never c

annotations.note: No divisibility-by-3 lemma for Pythagorean triples found.

anchor.snippet: Exactly one of a , b is divisible by 4

annotations.label: Exactly one of a,b,c divisible by 5

anchor.snippet: Exactly one of a , b , c is divisible by 5
The largest number that always divides abc is 60
Any odd number of the form 2 m +1 , where m is an integer and m >1 , can be the odd leg of a primitive Pythagorean triple
only even numbers divisible by 4 can be the even leg of a primitive Pythagorean triple

annotations.label: Hypotenuse form 4n+1

anchor.snippet: The hypotenuse c (which is always odd) is the sum of two squares

annotations.note: Sum-of-two-squares facts (`Nat.Prime.sq_add_sq`, `Nat.eq_sq_add_sq_iff`) give the general result; not specialized to hypotenuses.

annotations.label: Area is congruent number divisible by 6

anchor.snippet: is a congruent number

annotations.note: Congruent numbers are not defined in Mathlib.

anchor.snippet: In every Pythagorean triangle, the radius of the incircle and the radii of the three excircles are positive integers

annotations.label: Circumdiameter equals hypotenuse

anchor.snippet: the converse of Thales' theorem says that the diameter of the circumcircle equals the hypotenuse

annotations.note: Not formalized in this Pythagorean-triangle form.

anchor.snippet: When the area of a Pythagorean triangle is multiplied by the curvatures of its incircle and 3 excircles
the radius of the outer Soddy circle of any right triangle is equal to its semiperimeter
Only two sides of a primitive Pythagorean triple can be simultaneously prime

annotations.label: Only one side can be a perfect power

anchor.snippet: only one side can be an integer of perfect power

annotations.label: Fermat's right triangle theorem

anchor.snippet: There are no Pythagorean triangles in which the hypotenuse and one leg are the legs of another Pythagorean triangle

annotations.note: `fermatLastTheoremFour` proves FLT for n=4 via the standard infinite-descent argument equivalent to Fermat's right-triangle theorem, though not stated in that geometric form.

anchor.snippet: Each primitive Pythagorean triangle has a ratio of area
No primitive Pythagorean triangle has an integer altitude from the hypotenuse

annotations.label: Primitive triples form a rooted ternary tree

anchor.snippet: The set of all primitive Pythagorean triples forms a rooted ternary tree in a natural way

annotations.note: The Berggren tree structure is not formalized.

annotations.label: Acute angles not rational degrees

anchor.snippet: Neither of the acute angles of a Pythagorean triangle can be a rational number of degrees

annotations.note: Niven-type irrationality of Pythagorean angles is not formalized.

annotations.label: Integers not 2 mod 4 are in primitive triple

anchor.snippet: Every integer greater than 2 that is not congruent to 2 mod 4
Every integer greater than 2 is part of a primitive or non-primitive Pythagorean triple

annotations.label: Hypotenuse and longest leg differ by one

anchor.snippet: There exist infinitely many Pythagorean triples in which the hypotenuse and the longest leg differ by exactly one

annotations.label: Hypotenuse and longest leg differ by two

anchor.snippet: There exist infinitely many primitive Pythagorean triples in which the hypotenuse and the longest leg differ by exactly two
for every integer k > 0 , there exist infinitely many primitive Pythagorean triples in which the hypotenuse and the odd leg differ by
There exist infinitely many Pythagorean triples in which the two legs differ by exactly one
there exist k Pythagorean triples with different hypotenuses and the same area

annotations.label: k primitive triples with same leg

anchor.snippet: there exist at least k different primitive Pythagorean triples with the same leg
there exist at least k different Pythagorean triples with the same hypotenuse
If c = p e is a prime power , there exists a primitive Pythagorean triple

annotations.label: Hypotenuse characterization by prime factors

anchor.snippet: a positive integer c is the hypotenuse of a primitive Pythagorean triple if and only if each prime factor of c is congruent to 1 modulo 4

annotations.note: The corresponding sum-of-two-squares characterization is in Mathlib; the link to primitive Pythagorean hypotenuses is not made explicit.

anchor.snippet: There exist infinitely many Pythagorean triples with square numbers for both the hypotenuse
There exist non-primitive Pythagorean triangles with integer altitude from the hypotenuse

annotations.label: Correspondence: rational points and primitive triples

anchor.section: Rational points on a unit circle
anchor.snippet: the rational points on the circle are in one-to-one correspondence with the primitive Pythagorean triples

annotations.note: `circleEquivGen` parametrizes the unit circle and underlies the classification; the explicit 1-1 correspondence with primitive triples is not packaged as such.

annotations.label: Parametric equation of unit circle and Euclid's formula

anchor.section: Rational points on a unit circle
anchor.snippet: The unit circle may also be defined by a parametric equation

annotations.note: `circleEquivGen` is exactly the rational parametrization `x ↦ (2x/(1+x²), (1-x²)/(1+x²))` used to derive Euclid's formula.

annotations.label: Stereographic projection establishes rational correspondence

anchor.snippet: This establishes that each rational point of the x -axis goes over to a rational point of the unit circle

annotations.note: `circleEquivGen` provides the bijection (over `ℚ`) realizing the stereographic correspondence.

annotations.label: Unit circle is a rational curve

anchor.snippet: the algebraic variety of rational points on the unit circle is birational to the affine line over the rational numbers

annotations.note: The birationality statement is not formalized.

annotations.label: 1-to-1 mapping rationals to primitive triples

annotations.note: `circleEquivGen` gives the rational bijection; an explicit `ℚ ≃ {primitive triples}` map is not isolated.

annotations.label: Factoring in Gaussian integers

anchor.snippet: The right-hand side of the Pythagorean theorem may be factored in Gaussian integers

annotations.note: The Gaussian integers and their norm are formalized; the specific Pythagorean factorization `(a+bi)(a-bi)=c²` is not isolated as a lemma.

annotations.label: z and z* are squares of Gaussian integers

anchor.snippet: The two factors z := a + bi and z* := a − bi of a primitive Pythagorean triple each equal the square of a Gaussian integer

annotations.note: Not formalized as a Mathlib lemma.
